name: San José
summary: San José is Costa Rica's bustling capital and main transit hub, currently experiencing increased crime levels despite the country's overall peaceful reputation. While violent crime targeting tourists is relatively uncommon, petty crime including theft and pickpocketing is a significant concern, particularly in downtown areas and on public transportation. The city maintains good infrastructure and healthcare services, but visitors should exercise heightened awareness, especially at night.
quickVerdict: San José serves as most visitors' entry point to Costa Rica but presents moderate safety challenges as Central America's crime rates rise. The downtown core is generally safe during daylight hours but becomes riskier after dark, with petty crime being the primary concern for tourists. Most serious violent crimes are drug-related and don't typically target visitors. With proper precautions - avoiding flashy displays of wealth, staying in well-lit areas, and using official transportation - travelers can safely navigate the capital while accessing its cultural attractions and excellent healthcare facilities.

neighborhoods.name: Escazú
neighborhoods.description: Upscale suburb west of downtown favored by expats and wealthy locals, featuring modern shopping centers, international restaurants, and quality healthcare facilities like Hospital CIMA. Well-patrolled with good lighting and infrastructure.

neighborhoods.name: Rohrmoser
neighborhoods.description: Safe residential area near the airport popular with business travelers and diplomats. Features embassies, upscale hotels, and shopping areas with good security presence and lighting.

neighborhoods.name: Barrio Escalante
neighborhoods.description: Hip cultural district with art galleries, cafes, and restaurants. Generally safe during the day with active foot traffic, though caution needed at night on quieter side streets.

neighborhoods.name: Downtown Core
neighborhoods.description: Main business and tourist area around Teatro Nacional and Central Market. Busy and relatively safe during daylight hours but becomes concerning after dark with increased petty crime risk.

neighborhoods.name: Coca-Cola Area
neighborhoods.description: Major bus terminal district with heavy transient traffic. High concentration of pickpockets, street vendors, and opportunistic crime. Exercise extreme caution with belongings and avoid lingering unnecessarily.

neighborhoods.name: Pavas
neighborhoods.description: Industrial and residential area west of downtown experiencing rising crime rates. Some safer pockets exist near shopping centers, but generally avoid walking alone, especially at night.

scams.name: Fake Taxi/Bus Information
scams.description: Airport taxi drivers or individuals near bus stations falsely claim buses aren't running, seats are sold out, or hotels are overbooked to sell expensive alternative transportation or accommodation.
scams.howToAvoid: Always verify information directly at official bus stations or hotel reception. Use only official airport taxis with proper markings and set rates.

scams.name: Currency Exchange Rate Manipulation
scams.description: Businesses use outdated exchange rates (around 500 colones per USD instead of current rates near 610+) when tourists pay in US dollars, pocketing the difference as extra profit.
scams.howToAvoid: Know the current exchange rate before traveling. Pay in local colones when possible or verify posted exchange rates at establishments.

scams.name: ATM Skimming and Surveillance
scams.description: Thieves install card readers on ATMs or watch for withdrawal amounts, then follow victims to rob them. Some criminals pose as helpful strangers offering assistance at machines.
scams.howToAvoid: Use ATMs inside banks or busy shopping centers during daylight. Cover your PIN entry, check for unusual attachments, and vary your cash withdrawal locations.

scams.name: Friendly Stranger Approach
scams.description: English-speaking men approach tourists claiming to be from the US, strike up conversations, then invite victims for drinks or to explore areas where accomplices can rob or drug them.
scams.howToAvoid: Be polite but cautious with overly friendly strangers. Decline invitations to secondary locations and never leave drinks unattended.

soloFemale.overview: San José is moderately safe for solo female travelers with proper precautions, though it requires more vigilance than Costa Rica's tourist destinations. The city experiences typical urban safety challenges, with petty crime being the primary concern rather than gender-specific threats. Costa Rican culture is generally respectful toward women, and sexual harassment is less common than in some other Central American capitals. However, solo women should avoid walking alone after dark, particularly in downtown areas and around bus terminals.
tips: Stay in well-reviewed accommodations in safer neighborhoods like Escazú or Rohrmoser rather than budget hostels in downtown areas
Use official red taxis or ride-share apps instead of walking after dark, and always verify driver identity before entering vehicles
Dress conservatively and avoid flashy jewelry or electronics that could attract unwanted attention from petty thieves
Keep emergency contacts readily available and inform someone of your daily plans when exploring the city alone

nightSafety.overview: San José becomes significantly less safe after dark, with most safety incidents involving tourists occurring during evening hours. The downtown core, while busy during the day, sees increased criminal activity at night as foot traffic decreases and lighting becomes inadequate in many areas. Even neighborhoods considered safe during daylight require caution after sunset, and walking alone is strongly discouraged anywhere in the city.
tips: Use official taxis or ride-share services for any nighttime movement rather than walking, even for short distances
Stick to well-lit main streets and avoid shortcuts through parks or quiet side streets after dark
If staying downtown, return to your accommodation by 8-9 PM and avoid venturing out again until morning
Keep nighttime activities to upscale areas like Escazú where security presence is stronger and lighting is better

transport.metro: San José lacks a metro system. Public buses are the main urban transport but can be crowded and targets for pickpocketing. Red Cross buses serve major routes and are generally safer than smaller operators.
transport.rideshare: Uber operates informally (technically illegal but widely used) and is often safer and more reliable than taxis. Drivers may ask you to sit in front to avoid police detection. Service is good in central areas but limited in outlying neighborhoods.
transport.taxis: Official red taxis with yellow triangles and meters are the safest option. Ensure meters are running and avoid unmarked vehicles. Orange taxis serve the airport with fixed rates. Tourist police recommend using hotel-arranged taxis when possible.
transport.tips: Take photos of taxi license plates before entering. Avoid buses after dark and keep belongings secure. For airport transfers, use official shuttle services or pre-arranged transportation rather than improvising upon arrival.

customs: Greet with firm handshakes and eye contact; close friends may exchange cheek kisses, especially women greeting anyone and men greeting women
Embrace 'Pura Vida' philosophy - meaning 'pure life' - used as greeting, goodbye, or expression of contentment, reflecting the laid-back Costa Rican lifestyle
Practice 'La Hora Tica' (Tico Time) - expect 15-30 minute delays for social events as punctuality is relaxed, though business meetings require timeliness
Dress neatly and conservatively, avoiding shorts and flip-flops in urban areas; Costa Ricans take pride in appearance even for casual occasions
Show respect for family values and Catholicism; avoid public displays of affection and dress modestly when visiting religious sites
Accept coffee graciously when offered as it's central to social interaction; refusing may be considered impolite in Costa Rican hospitality culture

health.overview: San José offers excellent healthcare by regional standards, with both public and private systems available. The city hosts some of Central America's best medical facilities, making it a medical tourism destination. Private hospitals provide care comparable to North American standards with English-speaking staff.
health.water: Tap water in San José is generally safe to drink and meets international standards. However, some travelers prefer bottled water during initial adjustment. Ice in restaurants and hotels is typically made from treated water and safe to consume.
health.vaccinations: No special vaccinations required for San José beyond routine immunizations. Yellow fever vaccination needed only if arriving from endemic countries in South America or Africa. Consider Hepatitis A and Typhoid for extended stays or rural travel.
health.altitude: San José sits at 1,200 meters (3,937 feet) elevation in Costa Rica's Central Valley. The tropical highland climate features year-round temperatures of 15-26°C (59-79°F) with dry season December-April and rainy season May-November.

emergency.usEmbassy: US Embassy San José: +506 2519-2000 (business hours), +506 8863-4895 (after hours emergencies). Located in Pavas area. For non-emergency services, visit during business hours Monday-Friday.

faq.q: Is San José safe for tourists?
faq.a: San José is moderately safe with proper precautions. Petty crime like pickpocketing is the main concern, especially downtown and around bus terminals. Violent crime against tourists is uncommon but increased caution is needed compared to Costa Rica's beach destinations.

faq.q: Is San José safe at night?
faq.a: San José becomes significantly less safe after dark. Downtown areas should be avoided at night, and walking alone is strongly discouraged anywhere in the city. Use official taxis or ride-share services for any nighttime movement.

faq.q: Is San José safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Solo female travelers can visit San José safely with heightened awareness. Stay in safer neighborhoods like Escazú, use official transportation, dress conservatively, and avoid walking alone after dark. Costa Rican culture is generally respectful toward women.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in San José?
faq.a: Avoid the Coca-Cola bus terminal area, downtown core after dark, and Pavas neighborhoods at night. Exercise extra caution around any bus stations, poorly lit side streets, and areas with heavy transient traffic.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in San José?
faq.a: Daytime bus travel is generally safe but watch for pickpockets. Avoid public buses at night. Official red taxis with meters are safer, and Uber (though technically illegal) is widely used and often more reliable than street taxis.
